figures.caption: Figure 1: DCT matrix transforms. The image shows matrix transforms of size 16 × 128. Each matrix is multiplied by the image to be compressed, activating different columns. Blue colors indicate columns with zero values, yellow indicates columns with unit values, and intermediate colors correspond to values between zero and one. The more yellow the columns, the greater the weight given to the corresponding image region.

Figure 15: Comparison of AQMP with TPE, AQMP with Random Sampler, MP without quadtree refinement, and JPEG for the handwritten image ‘H09’ of the International Document Image Binarization Contest (DIBCO) from the year 2010 [41].

Figure 16: Compression-rate vs SSIM curves for AQMP (left) and JPEG (right) across images of the USC-SIPI dataset. AQMP curves are obtained by sweeping hyperparameters along the Pareto front; JPEG curves by varying quality factors.

Figure 17: Compression-rate vs SSIM curves for AQMP (left) and JPEG (right) across images of DIBCO dataset. AQMP curves are obtained by sweeping hyperparameters along the Pareto front; JPEG curves by varying quality factors.

Figure 6: Illustrative cost function and associated distributions of best and worst hyperparameters (l(x) and g(x), respectively). Also the ratio l(x)/g(x) is graphed. The distributions are obtained after 50 steps with TPE Sampler.

Figure 9: Hyperparameter Optimization for Splash image (512x512). The image shows the tradeoff between the SSIM and compression ratio for the five hyperparameters. The red curve shows the Pareto front in each image, which establishes the optimal election for the tradeoff between SSIM and compression rate.

Figure 10: Hyperparameter Optimization for Airplane (U-2) iamge (1024x1024). The image shows the tradeoff between the SSIM and compression ratio for the five hyperparameters. The red curve shows the Pareto front in each image, which establishes the optimal election for the tradeoff between SSIM and compression rate.

Figure 5: Illustrative probability distribution built from a set of hyperparameters. Each hyperparameter taken in account is represented with a red tick and a gaussian is centered around each one to build the resulting distribution. With TPE two of these are built: one for the best hyperparameters and another for the worst.

Figure 7: Image compression results for the Clock.png image with resolution 256× 256 pixels. The image was taken from the USC-SIPI Image Database [8]. From left to right: original image, compressed images with SSIM 0.993 and CR 1.356, SSIM 0.798 and CR 5.059, SSIM 0.653 and 11.288.
Figure 12: Comparison of AQMP with TPE, AQMP with Random Sampler, MP without quadtree refinement, and JPEG for the San Francisco image.
Figure 11: Comparison of AQMP with TPE, AQMP with Random Sampler, MP without quadtree refinement, and JPEG for the Female image.

Figure 18: Compression-rate vs SSIM curves for AQMP (left) and JPEG (right) across images of DIV2K dataset. AQMP curves are obtained by sweeping hyperparameters along the Pareto front; JPEG curves by varying quality factors.

Figure 14: Comparison of AQMP with TPE, AQMP with Random Sampler, MP without quadtree refinement, and JPEG for the handwritten image ‘H02’ of the International Document Image Binarization Contest (DIBCO) from the year 2010 [41].
Figure 13: Comparison of AQMP with TPE, AQMP with Random Sampler, MP without quadtree refinement, and JPEG for the Splash image.

Figure 8: Hyperparameter Optimization for Clock image (256x256). The image shows the tradeoff between the SSIM and compression ratio for the five hyperparameters. The red curve shows the Pareto front in each image, which establishes the optimal election for the tradeoff between SSIM and compression rate.
Table 1: Compression results for Splash image. Each row has results that are in the Pareto front. Execution time is the time needed to compress and decompress an image.
